nativeRange.habitat: Leptastrea is found on shallow tropical reef flats, lagoons, and back-reef zones across the Indo-Pacific, where it tolerates a broad range of lighting and water flow conditions.

decorPreferences: stable surface — frag plug or rock rubble
space from neighbors to prevent chemical aggression

diet.notes: Leptastrea draws the majority of its nutrition from zooxanthellae — symbiotic algae that live within coral tissue and convert light into energy. Supplemental target feeding with small meaty foods such as micro-plankton, mysis shrimp, or brine shrimp has been shown to benefit growth and coloration. Offering food in the evening, when polyps are typically extended, is generally recommended.

goodWith: other peaceful LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive sweeper-tentacle corals placed in direct contact
fast-growing SPS corals that may overgrow it
large aggressive LPS (Torch, Hammer, Frogspawn) without adequate spacing

imageCandidates.notes: Photo (c) Alie / C.A. Kratzer, 1024x768. Sharp in-situ colony clearly showing the diagnostic tightly-packed small corallites with contrasting green centers (the 'ocellated' eye look) on a pale coenosteum; encrusting sheet growth habit well represented. Wild olive-brown coloration; no free-license aquarium/actinic shot exists.

Photo by Ryan McMinds, 3648x2736. In-situ reef shot of a full encrusting Leptastrea mound with characteristic low rounded profile and tightly-packed corallites; correct ID and growth habit. Slightly hazy underwater and dull tan coloration, so kept as a secondary whole-colony reference.

summary: The Leptastrea Coral (Leptastrea purpurea) is an encrusting large polyp stony coral widely regarded as one of the more beginner-friendly stony corals in the reef hobby, tolerating a broader range of lighting and flow conditions than most LPS and showing minimal aggression toward neighboring corals.
careNotes:
Leptastrea is a genus of massive, encrusting LPS corals that was reclassified in 2020 into its own family, Leptastreidae, having previously been grouped with the Faviidae. The most commonly traded species is Leptastrea purpurea, also known as the Ocellated Brain Coral, though other Leptastrea species appear in the trade under the same common name.

Key care notes:

• Growth habit: Leptastrea encrusts across available substrate rather than growing upright. Colonies tend to form low, rounded mounds or flat sheets with tightly packed, small corallites. Captive specimens typically reach 5–30 cm across depending on conditions and time kept.

• Lighting: Moderate PAR (generally cited as 75–200 PAR) is well-tolerated. Leptastrea adapts to a wide range of intensities and is notably less photosensitive than many other LPS corals. New specimens should be introduced at lower light levels and gradually moved to their preferred position over 1–2 weeks.

• Flow: Moderate, intermittent flow is generally advised — enough to keep detritus from settling on the coral's surface but not so strong as to damage extending polyps. Alternating wavemaker patterns tend to work well.

• Placement: Leptastrea is one of the more placement-flexible LPS corals. It can generally be placed anywhere in the aquarium that meets its moderate light and flow requirements, including the lower rockwork or substrate where PAR is lower. It will encrust onto whatever surface it contacts.

• Aggression: Leptastrea is broadly considered one of the lower-aggression LPS corals and does not produce the long sweeper tentacles characteristic of genera like Hammer or Frogspawn. Some sources note nighttime sweeper tentacle extension, but hobbyist consensus and broader care guides describe it as relatively peaceful — it has short sweepers that pose minimal risk to neighbors kept at a few centimeters' distance. Maintaining a buffer of at least 5–8 cm from neighboring corals is a reasonable precaution.

• Feeding: Leptastrea derives the majority of its nutrition from zooxanthellae (symbiotic algae living within its tissues that convert light into energy). Weekly supplemental target-feeding with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp or micro-plankton in the evening, when polyps are extended, has been reported to support faster growth. This is optional under good lighting conditions.

• Water chemistry: Standard reef parameters are required. Ensure alkalinity (kH) remains stable in the 8–12 dKH range.

• Color morphs: Leptastrea is widely bred in captivity and is available in diverse color morphs, including orange, green, red, purple, and patterned combinations. Trade names such as 'WWC Burning Leptastrea', 'Kermit the Frog', and 'John Deere Leptastrea' refer to named color morphs of the same species.

Leptastrea is broadly regarded by hobbyists as one of the most forgiving LPS corals for newer reefers, with a reputation for tolerating imperfect parameters better than many other stony corals.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation — commonly called fragging in the reef hobby — is the primary propagation method in captivity. Because Leptastrea grows as an encrusting sheet, frags are typically produced by cutting or chiseling pieces from the edge of an established colony and securing them on frag plugs. Pieces tend to attach to new surfaces readily and begin encrusting. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs but is not practiced in the home aquarium.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Leptastrea
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: family Leptastreidae (reclassified from Faviidae in 2020), order Scleractinia. Seven described species confirmed including L. purpurea as the nominal/most common. Indo-Pacific distribution confirmed. No husbandry parameters.
